caveats: a: No published occupation mix exists for six-digit NAICS 541490 or the narrowed repeat-service lens.
a: The estimate excludes already automated work and does not assume generated output is commercially acceptable.
rho: This is an implementation judgment, not an AI-capability forecast.
rho: Small studios may lack integration capacity, while standardized high-volume work may implement faster.
e: The injected firm count is not re-estimated here.
e: NAICS classification does not identify repeat revenue, control eligibility, or whether design is sold separately from goods.
s5: Owner age is only a potential succession indicator; it does not establish sale probability.
s5: The estimate excludes closures without a transferable operating business and internal reorganizations.
q: This is a commercial-retention judgment and excludes demand-volume effects and implementation constraints.
q: Revenue-model shares are unavailable for the narrowed lens.
d5: Employment is not constant-price service quantity and is national rather than lens-specific.
d5: The projection covers fashion designers, not all included specialized-design activities.
o: The boundary between software-assisted service and self-service may change quickly.
o: This quantity judgment is separate from labor implementation and client price capture.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: External manufacturers, brands, retailers, and wholesalers buy fixed-fee, retainer, or repeat project design, specification, material-selection, and development services.
lens.deviation_from_default: Narrowed for coherence because NAICS 541490 combines recurring product-design studios with episodic float and costume work; the latter does not share a repeat outsourced-service revenue model.
lens.excluded_activities: One-off event float and costume work, independent theatrical designers, manufacturers selling their own goods, captive design teams, nonrepeat consumer commissions, shells, and financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US outsourced fashion, footwear, jewelry, textile, and related product-design studios serving external business customers on repeat seasonal, collection, or product-development engagements.

caveats: No published occupation mix exists for six-digit NAICS 541490 or the narrowed repeat-service lens.
The estimate excludes already automated work and does not assume generated output is commercially acceptable.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted time-and-motion survey of the included studios, paired with payroll occupation codes and workflow-level generative-AI use logs.
a.rationale: The included work contains AI-exposed concept exploration, reference research, rough illustration, specifications, documentation, costing support, and coordination. O*NET also shows client discussion, material selection, sample review, testing, and artistic judgment, which limit substitution. The specialized-design sector occupation mix includes management, sales, project-management, office, and market-research roles in addition to designers.

d5.bridge: BLS measures US employment of fashion designers, an occupation rather than demand for the narrowed NAICS 541490 external-service basket. Its 2 percent 2024–2034 projection is transformed to an approximately 1 percent five-year quantity proxy. It may overstate service demand when firms internalize or automate work, and may understate demand for adjacent footwear, jewelry, textile, and repeat product-development services.
caveats: Employment is not constant-price service quantity and is national rather than lens-specific.
The projection covers fashion designers, not all included specialized-design activities.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year constant-price revenue or unit-demand series for independent fashion, footwear, jewelry, and textile design studios, separated from manufacturing and internal design labor.
d5.rationale: BLS projects fashion-designer employment growth of 2 percent from 2024 to 2034. That near-flat outlook supports a central constant-quality demand ratio close to one, with downside for reduced outsourcing and upside for repeat collection and product-development needs.

caveats: The injected firm count is not re-estimated here.
NAICS classification does not identify repeat revenue, control eligibility, or whether design is sold separately from goods.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level NAICS 541490 prospect list with revenue, employee count, ownership, customer concentration, and repeat-revenue fields from business-information vendors and management interviews.
e.rationale: Eligibility is limited to firms in the injected LMM band that actually deliver repeat external outsourced services. The NAICS definition includes several relevant design categories, but it also includes activities and ownership structures outside the lens.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541490 ($2,027,827,545) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541490 ($3,671,109,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $308,134 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541490); class <5: 2,871 firms, avg revenue/firm $385,018 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $60,255 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 302 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,501,623 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $235,004 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 131 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,384,664 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $529,700 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 57 firms, avg revenue/firm $12,240,281 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,915,604 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 16 firms, avg revenue/firm $43,541,688 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $6,814,274 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 9 firms, avg revenue/firm $30,497,444 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $4,772,850 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 82

caveats: The boundary between software-assisted service and self-service may change quickly.
This quantity judgment is separate from labor implementation and client price capture.
o.change_evidence: Customer interviews and renewal analysis testing willingness to buy AI-enabled operator-led design versus self-service tools, plus usage data on vendor-supplied design platforms.
o.rationale: Clients still require accountable operators to translate brand direction, select materials, validate samples, make production-aware tradeoffs, and coordinate approvals. Some routine concept, research, and visualization work can instead be self-served or supplied through software.

caveats: This is a commercial-retention judgment and excludes demand-volume effects and implementation constraints.
Revenue-model shares are unavailable for the narrowed lens.
q.change_evidence: Customer contracts, renewal histories, statement-of-work pricing, and win-loss interviews that identify fixed-fee, retainer, and time-based revenue shares.
q.rationale: Fixed-fee and retainer work can initially retain some efficiency gains, but repeat clients and competitive studios can demand lower fees, larger deliverables, or faster turnarounds. Hourly and project billing also make pass-through and renewal repricing material.

caveats: This is an implementation judgment, not an AI-capability forecast.
Small studios may lack integration capacity, while standardized high-volume work may implement faster.
rho.change_evidence: Pilot results from comparable fashion, footwear, jewelry, and textile studios showing task-level cycle time, rework, quality acceptance, and headcount effects after adoption.
rho.rationale: Over five years, studios can implement AI for ideation, research, briefs, administrative support, and first-pass visual or specification work, but client approval, brand originality, fit, material behavior, production constraints, and quality review retain human workflow gates.

caveats: Owner age is only a potential succession indicator; it does not establish sale probability.
The estimate excludes closures without a transferable operating business and internal reorganizations.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of closed lower-middle-market transactions and quality-of-earnings records for NAICS 541490, supplemented by owner-age and succession-intent interviews with eligible firms.
s5.rationale: A five-year control-transfer probability is bounded judgment because no observed transaction series isolates eligible LMM firms in this six-digit industry. Census makes owner-age tabulations obtainable for employer firms, but the published ABS owner-characteristic table is not a control-transfer measure.

principal_driver: AI can reduce effort in repeatable research, visualization, specification, and coordination around operator-led product design.
principal_weakness: Observed evidence does not isolate transferable repeat-service firms or their demand, pricing, and transaction behavior within NAICS 541490.
